318	**Do NOT write "owed iter-N+" in an objective when a recipe already exists** (in `analogies/`, the blueprint chapter, or a prior task result). That phrase signals the prover to stop after achieving the hard bar and not attempt the body. Writing it when a concrete proof sketch exists is artificial throttling — the exact thing the dispatch guidance says to avoid.
319	
320	Use "owed iter-N+" ONLY when the proof body has NO concrete route yet (the blueprint chapter is empty for that step, no analogies file exists, and the informal agent hasn't produced a usable sketch). In all other cases, write "attempt the body; recipe: <path/to/file> <section>" so the prover uses remaining budget on the body and leaves partial progress if stuck.
321	
322	Partial progress from a real attempt (a partial tactic block, a named sub-goal that compiles, a helper lemma that closes) is far more valuable to the next iter than a clean typed-sorry pin with no attempt. The prover stops when it's genuinely stuck, not when the hard-bar checkbox is ticked.
323	
324	## Mathlib gradient strategy
325	
326	When a sorry's body depends on a Mathlib lemma or definition that does not yet exist in Mathlib, the default response is to leave the body as a sorry and wait for a Mathlib upstream PR. This is slow and blocks all downstream work. Use the **Mathlib gradient** approach instead:
327	
328	1. **Identify the missing ingredient.** Name it precisely: "we need `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` for Dedekind extensions" or "we need `Finsupp.posPart` for ordered groups".
329	2. **Check if it's buildable from current Mathlib.** Use `archon-informal-agent.py` or `WebSearch` to find a proof using only today's Mathlib. Almost always possible for a single lemma.
330	3. **Assign the prover with `[prover-mode: mathlib-build]`** to formalize that single ingredient axiom-clean in the project file that needs it. One lemma per iter if needed. The mode's strict no-sorry invariant ensures the output is either clean code or a precise decomposition — no sorry pins. The goal: each step only uses Mathlib + things already axiom-clean in the project.
331	4. **Once the ingredient is axiom-clean, use `prove` mode to close the sorry** in the same or next iter.
332	
333	The invariant: every sorry body that is deferred must have EITHER (a) no known proof route (not yet in the literature, or requires genuinely novel mathematics) OR (b) a missing Mathlib ingredient that is itself the explicit next objective. A sorry gated on "waiting for Mathlib to add X" with no project-side build plan is a planning failure.
334	
335	This gradient approach converts the project from a chain of blocked sorries into a steady incremental flow where each iter adds axiom-clean content. It is especially important for algebraic geometry, where large swaths of Mathlib are absent — the project must build those swaths itself, one lemma at a time.
336	
337	## Verification
338	
339	The loop already runs deterministic checks each iter:
340	
341	- **Sorry count** — stamped into `meta.json` (before/mid/post prover). Do not re-count by hand.
342	- **Axiom check** — runs as part of the blueprint-doctor; new axioms surface in your injected findings block.
343	- **Blueprint consistency** — `sync_leanok` resolves `\lean{...}` against the project decls; the doctor catches broken `\ref` / `\uses`.
344	
345	What's left for you: spot-check inconsistent prover self-reports; act on every entry in the injected doctor findings (or document the deferral); reject any reported completion that left a real `sorry` or introduced a new axiom.
346	
347	## Decomposition strategy
348	
349	When a prover is stuck on a large theorem: read the chapter for sub-lemma structure (L1, L2, …); read related `references/` to align with the original proof; expand the chapter if too thin (dispatch a blueprint-writing or literature-fetching subagent from the catalog, or use `WebSearch`/`WebFetch` directly); assign one sub-lemma at a time; verify, then assign the next; record each sub-lemma's status in `PROGRESS.md`.
350	
351	## Soundness check before spending budget
352	
353	**Churning and unsoundness are different signals — the critic catches the first, only you catch the second.** A recurring blocker is not always a hard-but-true gap; sometimes the target `sorry` is a statement that is simply **false as written** (a missing hypothesis, a wrong quantifier, an unstated connectedness/finiteness assumption). Pouring prover budget into a false statement burns iterations forever — the prover correctly cannot close it, the progress-critic reports CHURNING, and everyone treats it as a Mathlib gap when the real fix is one word in the statement.
354	
355	So **before committing more than one iter of prover budget to a hard or recurring `sorry`** (a target the progress-critic flags as a repeated blocker, or any target you estimate at multiple iters / >~100 LOC), first spend a cheap pass trying to **DISPROVE** the statement:
356	
357	- Instantiate it on the smallest non-trivial models — finite, degenerate, or boundary cases (e.g. for an algebra claim: `B = k × k`, `B = ℚ(√2)`, the zero ring, a one-point space). Does any satisfy the hypotheses but violate the conclusion?
358	- If you can't see it yourself, dispatch the informal / mathlib-analogist subagent from your catalog with a directive that asks specifically for a **counterexample or a satisfiability sketch**, not a proof.
359	- Check whether the source the statement claims to follow actually states it with the same hypotheses (cite-and-read discipline applies — read the local source file).
360	
361	If a counterexample turns up, the statement (or a missing hypothesis) is the bug: fix the blueprint statement, mark the Lean declaration with a `% NOTE:` for review, and do NOT assign a prover to formalize the false version. If the disproof attempt fails, you've cheaply raised your confidence that the target is true — now spend the budget. Record the disproof attempt and its outcome in the iter sidecar so the next planner doesn't repeat it.
362	
363	## Prover modes
364	
365	The available prover modes and their selection criteria are injected above under **Available prover modes**. Each mode's `dispatcher_notes` tell you exactly when to use it.
366	
367	**Mode selection is a required step, not optional.** For every file you add to `## Current Objectives`:
368	
369	1. Read the `dispatcher_notes` for each available mode (injected above).
370	2. Pick the mode whose `dispatcher_notes` best match the file's situation.
371	3. If a non-default mode fits, tag the objective line: `[prover-mode: <name>]`.
372	4. If the default is correct, no tag is needed — but you must have consciously checked.
373	
374	## Multi-agent coordination
375	
376	Provers run in parallel — one per file. Number objectives clearly; each maps to exactly one `.lean` file. Reference the blueprint chapter alongside, and **list every ready sorry in that file that the prover should fill in this iter** — not just one:
377	
378	```markdown
379	## Current Objectives
380	
381	1. **`Core.lean`** — Fill sorries in `filter_convergence` (line 156), `filter_inv` (line 188), `filter_assoc` (line 211). Blueprint: `chapters/Core.tex` (`thm:filter_convergence`, `thm:filter_inv`, `thm:filter_assoc`).
382	2. **`Measure.lean`** — Fill sorry in `sigma_finite_restrict` (line 45). Blueprint: `chapters/Measure.tex`. [prover-mode: fine-grained]
383	3. **`ChartAlgebra.lean`** — Scaffold the file with declarations for `thm:chart_id`, `thm:chart_comp`, `thm:chart_inv` from the chapter; leave bodies as `sorry`. Blueprint: `chapters/ChartAlgebra.tex`. (File-skeleton dispatch — see below.)
384	```
385	
386	**Agent count = file count.** The dispatcher fans out one prover per file. When a file has multiple ready sorries, list ALL of them under that file's objective — the prover handles them sequentially within one lane. Splitting a multi-sorry file across iters is artificial throttling; one lane working sequentially on three sorries finishes faster than waiting two iters for three single-sorry lanes (the prover keeps its file context warm across sorries).
387	
388	**File-skeleton dispatches.** When a blueprint chapter is complete but the corresponding `.lean` file does not yet exist (or exists but is missing declarations the chapter introduced), it is a legitimate iter objective to dispatch a prover with directive *"scaffold `Foo.lean` with declarations for `thm:a`, `thm:b`, `thm:c` from the chapter; leave bodies as `sorry`; add the import + namespace boilerplate; do not attempt to prove anything yet"*. The next iter then fills the sorries. This is materially faster than one-iter-per-declaration scaffolding.
389	
390	**Mechanical-vs-deep partition.** Sorries split into two regimes:
391	- *Mechanical* — typeclass wiring, instance synthesis, ring-level algebra, simp/ring-tactic territory, definitional unfolding glue. A prover lane can comfortably close 3–6 of these in one iter (the attempt-cap permits each one a fresh budget). When the upcoming work is mechanical, load lanes aggressively.
392	- *Deep* — the load-bearing categorical / geometric / analytic argument. One per lane, often less. The prover may also legitimately spend an iter exploring without closing anything.
393	
394	Use this partition to decide how thickly to load each lane. Don't load a deep lane with three deep sorries; that just thrashes the prover's attempt budget across all three. Don't restrict a mechanical lane to one sorry "to keep it simple"; the prover wants the batched objectives.
395	
396	Balance difficulty so all provers finish around the same time. Avoid shallow / trivial objectives unless they unblock something downstream this iter. Don't artificially throttle — the prover prompt says "push as far as possible"; your objective list must give it room to.
397	
398	If a previous experiment is being restarted, check compilation status of every target `.lean` first. Prioritize files with sorries or compile errors; don't redo completed work.
399	
400	**Dispatch cap.** The runner refuses to fan out more than ~10 provers in a single iter (configurable via `--max-objectives`, default 10). Writing 15+ files into `## Current Objectives` is a planning failure, not a tooling limitation — even when the project has many open files, the right iter-level move is to pick the most urgent ≤10 (mechanical lanes counted) and defer the rest to the next iter. If the deterministic plan-validate guard truncates your list, the surplus is added to `USER_HINTS.md` so the next planner sees what got deferred. Don't rely on the safety net — pick the right ~10 the first time.
401	
402	**Blocked-deps filter.** Before dispatch, plan-validate also drops any objective whose transitive local imports failed the *previous* `lake build`. Reason: a prover assigned to `Downstream.lean` that imports `Upstream.lean`-which-doesn't-compile would fail to even load the file, burning API time for nothing. The blocked set is parsed from `.archon/last_lake_build.log`. There is one important exception: a blocked file that's *itself* an objective this iter is presumed-being-fixed — the planner is allowed to assign `Upstream.lean` and `Downstream.lean` together (the prover phase handles them in import order). When the filter drops files, they're listed in `USER_HINTS.md` with their specific blocking deps, so you can prioritize fixing the upstream files next iter. Best practice: when you see `## Build state` flagging compile errors, put those files at the top of `## Current Objectives` so the filter exempts the downstream lanes that depend on them.
403	
404	## Dependency graph
405	
406	Optional but cheap. Before scoping objectives, you may run:
407	
408	```
409	${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" . --format=summary
410	```
411	
412	It parses imports + chapter `\lean{...}` / `\uses{...}` / `\proves{...}` / markers and emits a project-wide view in under a second. Use it to order objectives — upstream files first, downstream files later.
413	
414	## Stage transitions
415	
416	Advance `PROGRESS.md` when all current-stage objectives are met:
417	
418	- `autoformalize` → `prover` (all statements formalized)
419	- `prover` → `polish` (all sorries filled and verified)
420	- `polish` → `COMPLETE` (proofs clean, compile)

38	**A.1.c.SubT — group law on locally-trivial iso-classes.** `Pic X` = the commutative group of
39	iso-classes of locally-trivial (invertible) `𝒪_X`-modules under ⊗. The `CommGroup` is built BY HAND,
40	feeding each group axiom a single *existence-of-iso* — `one_mul`/`mul_one` the unitors, `mul_assoc`
41	the associator, `mul_comm` the braiding, inverses the dual; coherence/pentagon is never invoked.

43	`Skeleton (Module.Invertible R)`, reading its `CommGroup` off the coherent `MonoidalCategory` it has
44	over a FIXED ring. For the VARYING structure sheaf that coherent `MonoidalCategory (X.Modules)` is
45	unavailable, so `Skeleton`/`monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal` cannot be reused — hence the by-hand assembly. Consequently the whole monoidal-category

74	rigidity — substrate partial, PAUSED (USER).

1	# Analogy: does the scheme Picard group law need a coherent monoidal category, or only existence-of-isos?
2	
3	## Mode
4	api-alignment
5	
6	## Slug
7	ts-picard-direct-216
8	
9	## Iteration
10	216
11	
12	## Question
13	Does the commutative-group structure on locally-trivial iso-classes genuinely
14	require only existence-of-associativity-iso (NOT a coherent natural associator,
15	NO pentagon, NO localizer monoidality)? Is the locally-trivial associator
16	buildable directly (à la the in-file `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial`) from
17	`tensorObj_restrict_iso`, making the general-site whiskering apparatus
18	(`isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W`, `(J.W).IsMonoidal`, d.1/d.2 stalk-⊗)
19	vestigial dead code?
20	
21	## Project artifact(s)
22	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean:775-787` — §2 docstring: full `MonoidalCategory (X.Modules)` deliberately NOT built; group law from 4 existence-of-iso lemmas "mirroring Mathlib's `CommRing.Pic`".
23	- `…:903-946` — `tensorObj_assoc_iso`, built via ROUTE (d) whiskering (Steps 1/3 = `W_whiskerRight/Left_of_W`, Step 2 = `a.mapIso α`).
24	- `…:301-601` — the whiskering apparatus: `isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W` (**open sorry, L546**, the 6-iter blocker), `W_whiskerLeft/Right_of_W`, `isIso_sheafification_map_of_W`, the stalk-linear d.1 section.
25	- `…:1004-1108` — `tensorObj_restrict_iso` (sorry L1082, the H1 funnel) + `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial` (GREEN modulo restrict_iso) — the gluing template the planner cites.
26	
27	## Decisions identified
28	
29	### Decision 1: group-law substrate — coherent monoidal category vs. existence-of-iso
30	
31	- **Mathlib idiom**: `CommRing.Pic R := Shrink (Skeleton (SemimoduleCat R))ˣ`
32	  (`Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean:407-412`). The `CommGroup` comes from
33	  `(…)ˣ` (units) of `Skeleton.instCommMonoid`, which is
34	  `commMonoidOfSkeletalBraided` over `monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal`
35	  (`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean:38-47, 80-81`). **The monoid
36	  laws are literally:**
37	  ```
38	  one_mul  X     := hC ⟨λ_ X⟩
39	  mul_one  X     := hC ⟨ρ_ X⟩
40	  mul_assoc X Y Z := hC ⟨α_ X Y Z⟩      -- hC : Skeletal C, i.e. (X ≅ Y) → X = Y
41	  mul_comm  X Y  := hC ⟨β_ X Y⟩
42	  ```
43	  Each axiom is discharged by feeding ONE iso (`⟨α_ X Y Z⟩`, a `Nonempty`) to
44	  `Skeletal`. The pentagon/triangle/hexagon coherence carried by the
45	  `MonoidalCategory` typeclass is **never invoked** in any monoid-law proof.
46	  So Mathlib's *own* Picard-group construction logically consumes only the
47	  existence of the associator/unitor/braiding isos. (It gets them "for free"
48	  from the bundled `MonoidalCategory ModuleCat` instance, but the laws don't
49	  touch coherence.)
50	- **Project's current path**: builds the group law directly on iso-classes of
51	  locally-trivial modules from four existence-of-iso lemmas (`tensorObj_assoc_iso`,
52	  `tensorObj_left/right_unitor`, `tensorObj_braiding`, `exists_tensorObj_inverse`),
53	  with no `MonoidalCategory (X.Modules)` instance.
54	- **Gap**: identical-in-substance. The project re-derives exactly the proof
55	  obligations of `monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal` (one `Nonempty(≅)` per axiom). It
56	  cannot literally reuse `Skeleton.instMonoid` because that needs a full
57	  `MonoidalCategory (X.Modules)` instance — whose pentagon fields would require
58	  the natural coherent associator, i.e. strictly MORE than the blocked route
59	  already wants. Mathlib has no scheme-level Picard idiom (the file's own TODO
60	  L59-61 lists "connect to invertible sheaves on Spec R" as future work).
61	- **Cost of divergence**: none. Building a coherent `MonoidalCategory` to use
62	  `Skeleton` would be *more* expensive, not less.
63	- **Verdict**: **NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL** — scheme-level construction absent
64	  from Mathlib; the project's design is correctly aligned to the ring-level
65	  idiom's actual substrate (existence-of-iso, not coherence). Planner claim #1
66	  CONFIRMED.
67	
68	### Decision 2: associator route — general-site whiskering vs. `tensorObj_restrict_iso`
69	
70	- **Mathlib idiom**: the only thing `monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal` ever needs from
71	  the associator is `⟨α_ X Y Z⟩` — a single iso. It builds NO localizer
72	  monoidality, NO `(J.W).IsMonoidal`, NO stalkwise-tensor commutation. The
73	  whole `(J.W).IsMonoidal`/whiskering apparatus has no counterpart in the
74	  Mathlib Picard pipeline.
75	- **Project's current path**: `tensorObj_assoc_iso` is built via ROUTE (d):
76	  transport the global presheaf associator `α` through sheafification, using
77	  `W_whiskerLeft/Right_of_W` to pull the inner sheafification out of the nested
78	  `tensorObj (tensorObj M N) P`. Those bottom out in
79	  `isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W` (open sorry), whose two residuals are
80	  the Mathlib-absent d.1/d.2 stalk-⊗-over-a-varying-ring machinery (~300 LOC of
81	  apparatus L301-601 hangs off this).
82	- **Gap**: divergent-with-cost. The whiskering route is an over-engineered way
83	  to obtain a datum (`Nonempty((M⊗N)⊗P ≅ M⊗(N⊗P))`) that a lighter route
84	  supplies. For **locally-trivial** M,N,P (the only case the group law touches)
85	  the associator is obtainable from `tensorObj_restrict_iso` alone — the same
86	  single ingredient `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial`, the unitors, the inverse, and
87	  the bifunctor all already funnel through. Closing `tensorObj_restrict_iso`
88	  (H1: presheaf-level `pushforwardPushforwardAdj` / sectionwise extension of
89	  scalars) thus retires the *entire* whiskering+stalk apparatus as dead code.
90	- **Cost of divergence (current route)**: a 6-iter-stuck sorry plus ~300 LOC of
91	  whiskering/stalk scaffolding, whose terminal dependency (d.2 stalk-⊗ over the
92	  varying ring) is the project's own "genuinely Mathlib-absent (largest piece)"
93	  (L543-544). All of it avoidable.
94	- **CAVEAT the planner must budget (do not under-scope)**: "build the associator
95	  directly by gluing, mirroring `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial`" understates the
96	  work. `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial` proves a **Prop** — `∀ x, ∃ U, Nonempty(…≅𝒪_U)`
97	  — purely pointwise, with NO overlap compatibility. The associator is **global
98	  data**: a single iso of sheaves on all of X. You cannot assemble it from
99	  pointwise-chosen trivialization isos (arbitrary local isos do not agree on
100	  overlaps). The correct construction:
101	  on each open `Uᵢ` of a common trivialising cover, form the **canonical**
102	  ```
103	  ((M⊗N)⊗P)|Uᵢ ≅ (M|⊗N|)⊗P|  --[restrict_iso, twice]
104	              ≅ M|⊗(N|⊗P|)     --[canonical PRESHEAF associator on restrictions]
105	              ≅ (M⊗(N⊗P))|Uᵢ  --[restrict_iso, twice]
106	  ```
107	  Because `tensorObj_restrict_iso` is natural and the presheaf associator is
108	  natural/canonical, these local isos **do** agree on overlaps, so a
109	  "Hom-of-sheaves-is-a-sheaf" gluing assembles the global iso. This needs:
110	  (i) `tensorObj_restrict_iso` (the named funnel), (ii) the canonical presheaf
111	  associator (`PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator`, already
112	  available — it is Step 2 of the current route), and (iii) a gluing-of-isos
113	  lemma + the overlap-naturality proof. Item (iii) is a real, bounded cost — but
114	  it does NOT touch `(J.W).IsMonoidal`, d.1, or d.2. (One could even skip the
115	  cover entirely and glue the canonical local isos directly via the sheaf
116	  condition on internal Hom, but the cover form matches the in-file template.)
117	- **Verdict**: **ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB** — drop the whiskering/`(J.W).IsMonoidal`/
118	  stalk route; build `tensorObj_assoc_iso` from `tensorObj_restrict_iso`
119	  + canonical presheaf associator + gluing. Planner claims #2/#3 CONFIRMED with
120	  the gluing caveat.
121	
122	## Recommendation
123	
124	The planner is right on all three counts, with one scoping correction. (1) The
125	group law needs only existence-of-iso, exactly as Mathlib's `Skeleton`
126	monoid-law proofs show (`mul_assoc := hC ⟨α⟩`); the project's no-`MonoidalCategory`
127	design is the *correct* alignment, and building a coherent monoidal category to
128	reuse `Skeleton` would be strictly harder. (2)+(3) The general-site whiskering
129	apparatus (`isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W` and its `(J.W).IsMonoidal` /
130	d.1 / d.2 dependents, ~300 LOC + the 6-iter sorry) is **vestigial**: it exists
131	only to realise an associator datum that, for locally-trivial M,N,P, follows
132	from `tensorObj_restrict_iso` — the single ingredient through which
133	`tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial`, the unitors, and the inverse already pass. The
134	project is NOT condemned to build d.2 / `(J.W).IsMonoidal`. Refactor: re-route
135	`tensorObj_assoc_iso` through `tensorObj_restrict_iso` and delete the whiskering
136	section; concentrate all remaining effort on the **single** linchpin
137	`tensorObj_restrict_iso` (H1 presheaf `pushforwardPushforwardAdj`). Budget the
138	associator's gluing-of-compatible-local-isos step (the one piece "mirror
139	`tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial`" hides, because that lemma is a Prop and the
140	associator is global data) — a bounded cost, not a Mathlib gap.

1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
80	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
81	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
82	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
83	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
84	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
85	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
88	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
89	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
90	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
91	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
92	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
93	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
94	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
95	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
96	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
97	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
98	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
99	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
100	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
101	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
102	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
103	supplied.
104	
105	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
106	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
107	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
108	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
109	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
110	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
111	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
112	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
113	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
114	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
115	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
116	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
117	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
118	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
119	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
120	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
121	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
122	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
123	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
124	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
125	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
126	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
127	avoids. The
128	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
129	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
130	The whole \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk
131	apparatus is therefore vestigial for the group law and off the critical path.
132	
133	\section{Mathlib API survey}
134	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
135	
136	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
137	(\texttt{b80f227}) were originally assembled into a single \emph{realization route}
138	(route~(e)): obtaining a full monoidal structure on the substrate by instantiating
139	Mathlib's abstract localization-of-monoidal-categories API rather than
140	hand-assembling the associator. That route is \emph{superseded}. The group law on
141	iso-classes never consumes a coherent monoidal category, and the associator is
142	obtained by direct gluing through \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, so the
143	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance on which route~(e) hinges is never required.
144	The survey below is retained as a Mathlib-API reference and to record why the route
145	is vestigial; the four Mathlib facts are all verified against the on-disk Mathlib
146	source.
147	
148	\paragraph{(i) The presheaf-of-modules monoidal category.} Mathlib's
149	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal} constructs a
150	monoidal-category instance
151	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\) on
152	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,
153	\mathtt{RingCat})\), with the sectionwise relative tensor
154	\[
155	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
156	\]
157	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct` /
158	% `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`, in
159	% `Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean` (L104, L125)
160	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean).
161	% SOURCE QUOTE:
162	% "noncomputable instance monoidalCategoryStruct :
163	%     MonoidalCategoryStruct (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) where
164	%   tensorObj := tensorObj
165	%   whiskerLeft _ _ _ g := tensorHom (𝟙 _) g
166	%   whiskerRight f _ := tensorHom f (𝟙 _)
167	%   ...
168	%   tensorUnit := unit _
169	%   associator M₁ M₂ M₃ := isoMk (fun _ ↦ α_ _ _ _) ...
170	% noncomputable instance monoidalCategory :
171	%     MonoidalCategory (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) where ..."
172	together with associator, left/right unitors and braiding making it symmetric
173	monoidal. The base presheaf of rings \(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,
174	\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat}\) is exactly the project's
175	\(\mathtt{Sheaf.val}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) carrier (equal by \(\mathtt{rfl}\)),
176	so this monoidal structure applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
177	\(\mathcal{O}_X\) --- no project work. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
178	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
179	
180	\paragraph{(ii) The localization-of-monoidal-categories API.} Mathlib's
181	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.Basic} provides the class
182	\(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) and the construction
183	\(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\). The class records exactly
184	the whiskering-stability data:
185	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `CategoryTheory.MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal` and
186	% `CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal`, in
187	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean` (L44, L86, L440)
188	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean).
189	% SOURCE QUOTE:
190	% "class IsMonoidal : Prop extends W.IsMultiplicative where
191	%   whiskerLeft (X : C) {Y₁ Y₂ : C} (g : Y₁ ⟶ Y₂) (hg : W g) : W (X ◁ g)
192	%   whiskerRight {X₁ X₂ : C} (f : X₁ ⟶ X₂) (hf : W f) (Y : C) : W (f ▷ Y)
193	%  ...
194	% def LocalizedMonoidal (L : C ⥤ D) (W : MorphismProperty C)
195	%     [W.IsMonoidal] [L.IsLocalization W] {unit : D} (_ : L.obj (𝟙_ C) ≅ unit) := D
196	%  ...
197	% noncomputable instance :
198	%     MonoidalCategory (LocalizedMonoidal L W ε) where ..."
199	Given \([\MonoidalCategory\,C]\), \([W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}]\), a localization
200	functor \(L : C \to D\) with \([L.\mathtt{IsLocalization}\,W]\) and an isomorphism
201	\(\varepsilon : L(\mathbf{1}_C) \cong \mathtt{unit}\), the localized category
202	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\,L\,W\,\varepsilon\) is a \(\MonoidalCategory\) with
203	\emph{all} coherence (associator, unitors, pentagon, triangle, naturalities)
204	derived. There is \emph{no} \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) anywhere in the
205	hypothesis chain.
206	
207	\paragraph{(iii) The proof template for $W$-monoidality.} Mathlib's
208	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW} proves
209	\((J.W\,(A := A)).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) \emph{stalkwise} for presheaves valued in a
210	\emph{fixed} monoidal concrete category \(A\) (i.e.\ \(C^{op} \to A\)), via the
211	conservative-family characterisation \(\mathtt{hP.W\_iff}\),
212	\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.map\_tensor}\) and typeclass inference:
213	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `CategoryTheory.ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W`
214	% and the `(J.W).IsMonoidal` instance, in
215	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean` (L48, L57)
216	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean).
217	% SOURCE QUOTE:
218	% "lemma ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W
219	%     [J.HasSheafCompose (forget A)] :
220	%     (J.W (A := A)).IsMonoidal :=
221	%   .mk' _ (fun f g hf hg ↦ by
222	%     simp only [hP.W_iff (A := A)] at hf hg ⊢
223	%     intro Φ
224	%     rw [Functor.Monoidal.map_tensor]
225	%     infer_instance)"
226	This is the \emph{template} for the proof technique used in route~(e), but it does
227	\emph{not} apply directly to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\): modules over a
228	\emph{varying} ring are not of the form \(C^{op} \to A\) for a fixed monoidal
229	\(A\).
230	
231	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e), superseded.} The substrate
232	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
233	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
234	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
235	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Route~(e)
236	proposed to obtain a \emph{full} monoidal structure on
237	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
238	by applying \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) (ii) to the already-monoidal category (i),
239	which would require the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (its two
240	whiskering-stability fields \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, with load-bearing
241	local-injectivity half \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}). This is
242	\emph{not} how the group law is realized. The group law consumes only the
243	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms, and the associator is built
244	directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through
245	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}); it never invokes
246	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), a coherent monoidal category, or the stalkwise
247	whiskering apparatus. The route~(e) instance is therefore vestigial and off the
248	critical path.
249	
250	The stalk-infrastructure analysis that route~(e) turned on is recorded here for
251	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
252	is already present in Mathlib: the file
253	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
254	space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
255	\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
256	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
257	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
258	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
259	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
260	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
261	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
262	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
263	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
264	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. Because the group law
265	is realized through the direct-gluing associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso},
266	via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and never through
267	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), none of this stalk apparatus is on the critical path;
268	it is retained only to document the superseded route.
269	
270	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
271	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
272	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
273	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
274	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
275	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
276	independently of any upstream timing.
277	
278	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
279	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
280	
281	\begin{definition}
282	
283	\leanok
284	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
285	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
286	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
287	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
288	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
289	  \[
290	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
291	  \]
292	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
293	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
294	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
295	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
296	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
297	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
298	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
299	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
300	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
301	  canonical compatibility
302	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
303	     \;\cong\;
304	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
305	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
306	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
307	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
308	
309	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
310	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
311	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
312	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
313	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
314	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
315	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
316	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
317	\end{definition}
318	
319	\begin{lemma}
320	
321	\leanok
322	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
323	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
324	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
325	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
326	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
327	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
328	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
329	  \[
330	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
331	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
332	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
333	  \]
334	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
335	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
336	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
337	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
338	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
339	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
340	  \[
341	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
342	    \qquad
343	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
344	  \]
345	  the associator
346	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
347	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
348	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
349	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
350	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
351	\end{lemma}
352	
353	\begin{proof}
354	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
355	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
356	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
357	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
358	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
359	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
360	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
361	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
362	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
363	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
364	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
365	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
366	\end{proof}
367	
368	\begin{remark}
369	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
370	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
371	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
372	        lem:whisker_of_W}
373	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
374	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
375	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
376	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
377	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
378	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
379	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
380	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
381	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
382	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
383	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
384	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
385	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
386	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
387	
388	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
389	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
390	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
391	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
392	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
393	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
394	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
395	  fixed-base presheaves in
396	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
397	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
398	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
399	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). But this
400	  apparatus is not on the critical path at all: the group law is realized without
401	  it.
402	
403	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
404	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
405	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
406	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
407	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
408	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
409	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
410	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
411	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
412	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
413	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
414	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
415	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
416	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
417	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
418	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
419	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
420	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
421	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
422	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
423	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
424	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
425	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
426	\end{remark}
427	
428	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
429	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
430	
431	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
432	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
433	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
434	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
435	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
436	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
437	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
438	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
439	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
440	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
441	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
442	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
443	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
444	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
445	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
446	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
447	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
448	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
449	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
450	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
451	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
452	to the off-path route~(e).
453	
454	\begin{lemma}
455	
456	\leanok
457	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
458	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
459	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
460	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
461	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
462	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
463	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
464	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
465	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
466	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
467	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
468	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
469	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
470	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
471	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
472	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
473	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
474	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
475	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
476	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
477	  \[
478	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
479	      \;\;:=\;\;
480	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
481	  \]
482	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
483	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
484	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
485	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
486	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
487	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
488	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
489	\end{lemma}
490	
491	\begin{proof}
492	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
493	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
494	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
495	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
496	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
497	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
498	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
499	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
500	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
501	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
502	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
503	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
504	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
505	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
506	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
507	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
508	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
509	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
510	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
511	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
512	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
513	
514	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
515	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
516	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
517	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
518	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
519	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
520	\end{proof}
521	
522	\begin{lemma}
523	
524	\leanok
525	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
526	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
527	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
528	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
529	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
530	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
531	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
532	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
533	  morphism
534	  \[
535	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
536	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
537	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
538	  \]
539	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
540	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
541	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
542	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
543	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
544	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
545	\end{lemma}
546	
547	\begin{proof}
548	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
549	  % NOTE (review iter-216): the iter-216 prover MAKE-OR-BREAK finding refutes the
550	  % "free-cover avoids H1" guidance below. The sole consumer
551	  % `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial` applies `tensorObj_restrict_iso W.ι M N` to ARBITRARY
552	  % M N (the restrict is commuted past the SHEAFIFIED tensor before the triviality
553	  % witnesses eM, eN are used), so H1 (presheaf `pushforwardPushforwardAdj`) is on the
554	  % critical path and cannot be deferred. The free-cover paragraph (lines ~641-657) needs
555	  % a blueprint-writer correction before any prover re-attempts this lemma. See
556	  % task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-ts216.md (must-fix) and the iter-216 Lean
557	  % comment at TensorObjSubstrate.lean:1159-1176.
558	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
559	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
560	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
561	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
562	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
563	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
564	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
565	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
566	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
567	  isomorphism}
568	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
569	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
570	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
571	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
572	  therefore extension of scalars along
573	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
574	
575	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
576	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
577	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
578	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
579	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
580	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
581	  the abstract pullback functor.
582	
583	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
584	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
585	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
586	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
587	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
588	   \;\cong\;
589	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
590	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
591	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
592	  comparison
593	  \[
594	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
595	      \;\longrightarrow\;
596	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
597	      \;\otimes\;
598	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
599	  \]
600	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
601	
602	  \emph{Step 3 (the genuine residual: H1 alone).} Completing this comparison at the
603	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
604	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
605	  adjoint of pushforward and admits no sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the pinned
606	  commit. The closure passes through two presheaf-level ingredients, of which
607	  exactly one now remains open.
608	
609	  The first ingredient --- a strong-monoidal upgrade of
610	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-sheaf ring \emph{isomorphism}
611	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so that base change along the isomorphism commutes with
612	  \(\otimes\) --- is \emph{closed in this chapter} as
613	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
614	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}\)): for a ring isomorphism
615	  \(e : R \simeq S\) the canonical map \(a \otimes_R b \mapsto a \otimes_S b\) is an
616	  \(R\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R
617	  \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M
618	  \otimes_S N)\).
619	
620	  The \emph{sole remaining residual} is therefore the presheaf-level identification
621	  \[
622	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \;\cong\; \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
623	  \]
624	  via \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\). One exhibits a
625	  presheaf-level adjunction
626	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) and concludes
627	  that \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\), being a left adjoint of
628	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), is canonically isomorphic to the abstract left
629	  adjoint \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\). Here \(\beta\) is the structure map of the
630	  restriction functor, so that \((M|_f).\mathrm{val} =
631	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,M.\mathrm{val}\) definitionally;
632	  composing the resulting comparison with
633	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} yields the asserted isomorphism.
634	
635	  This presheaf-level adjunction is the presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
636	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\), and must be built from
637	  presheaf-level \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
638	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\). These two building blocks are \emph{Mathlib-absent}
639	  at the pinned commit: only the \emph{sheaf}-level versions exist
640	  (\texttt{Mathlib/.../SheafOfModules/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}), whereas
641	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardId}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) do already exist at the
642	  presheaf level. The depth of this sub-chain is at present uncertain.
643	
644	  This lemma is the single substrate linchpin of the relative Picard group law: the
645	  direct gluing construction of the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
646	  invokes it (twice per cover member) to descend to the canonical presheaf-level
647	  associator. Closing the H1 residual is therefore the sole remaining substrate
648	  obligation.
649	
650	  \emph{The make-or-break: only the free-cover case is on the critical path.} The
651	  statement above is proved here for \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
652	  \(M, N\), and in that generality the closure passes through the H1 residual
653	  (\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\)), whose
654	  presheaf-level building blocks are Mathlib-absent. But the associator
655	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes this comparison only on a \emph{trivialising
656	  cover}, where \(M\) and \(N\) restrict to \emph{free} modules. On that free cover
657	  the comparison is the elementary, structural one: sheaf-\(\otimes\) coincides with
658	  presheaf-\(\otimes\) on the free pieces, and restriction along the open immersion is
659	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{ring isomorphism} \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\),
660	  so the isomorphism follows from
661	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} alone, with \emph{no} appeal to the
662	  general presheaf-pushforward adjunction. The PRIMARY proof obligation of the
663	  construction is therefore the free-cover specialisation of this lemma, to be
664	  discharged \emph{without} H1; the arbitrary-module statement (with its H1 residual)
665	  is strictly stronger than the group law requires. As a fallback, should the
666	  free-cover case itself genuinely require the general adjunction H1, the by-hand
667	  route would have gained nothing over instantiating the coherent monoidal structure,
668	  and one would revert to comparing against the superseded route~(e)
669	  \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to \mathtt{Sheaf.monoidalCategory}\)
670	  (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}).
671	\end{proof}

673	\begin{lemma}
674	
675	\leanok
676	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with $\otimes$]
677	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
678	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}
679	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M, N\) be
680	  \(S\)-modules. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the tensor
681	  product: the canonical map
682	  \[
683	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N)
684	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
685	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \otimes_S N\bigr),
686	    \qquad a \otimes_R b \;\longmapsto\; a \otimes_S b,
687	  \]
688	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence. Equivalently, \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\) is
689	  \emph{strong} monoidal, not merely lax, when \(e\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}:
690	  base change along an isomorphism \(R \simeq S\) identifies the two ground rings, so
691	  the lax structure map of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) is invertible. The
692	  forward map is \(R\)-linear, but its inverse \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\)
693	  is only \emph{additive} (it is not \(S\)-linear), so the inverse is constructed as
694	  a homomorphism of additive groups and then verified to be the two-sided inverse of
695	  the forward \(R\)-linear map. This closes the strong-monoidal ingredient of
696	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, leaving the presheaf-level pushforward
697	  adjunction as the sole open residual there.
698	\end{lemma}
699	
700	\begin{lemma}
701	
702	\leanok
703	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
704	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
705	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
706	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
707	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
708	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
709	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
710	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
711	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
712	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
713	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
714	\end{lemma}
715	
716	\begin{proof}
717	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
718	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
719	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
720	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
721	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
722	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
723	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
724	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
725	\end{proof}

1247	\begin{lemma}
1248	
1249	\leanok
1250	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on locally trivial objects]
1251	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
1252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso}
1253	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1254	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
1255	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). There is an isomorphism
1256	  \[
1257	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
1258	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1259	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
1260	  \]
1261	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), natural in \(M, N, P\). The group law on
1262	  \(\otimes\)-iso-classes (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) consumes only
1263	  the \emph{existence} of this isomorphism, the proposition
1264	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \cong M \otimes_X (N
1265	  \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality coherence of it is
1266	  ever required.
1267	
1268	  The current Lean pin additionally carries \emph{locally trivial}
1269	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\), \cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial})
1270	  hypotheses on \(M, N, P\). The construction below produces the isomorphism for
1271	  arbitrary \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules; the hypotheses are retained only to match
1272	  the carrier the group law multiplies on, and are not used in the gluing.
1273	\end{lemma}
1274	
1275	\begin{proof}
1276	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1277	  % NOTE (review iter-216): this proof sketch (direct gluing, "no whiskering") does NOT
1278	  % match the current Lean pin. `tensorObj_assoc_iso` in TensorObjSubstrate.lean still
1279	  % uses the ROUTE-(d) three-step composite (`W_whiskerRight_of_W` / `W_whiskerLeft_of_W`
1280	  % / `isIso_sheafification_map_of_W` + the presheaf associator) and does NOT invoke
1281	  % `tensorObj_restrict_iso` at all. Per the iter-216 prover, the direct-gluing re-route
1282	  % was assessed but DEFERRED (it still transitively needs the open `tensorObj_restrict_iso`
1283	  % and ~150-250 LOC of overlap/amalgamation, with no honest-close gain). Blueprint-writer
1284	  % correction needed: align this sketch with the actual whiskering route, OR mark the
1285	  % direct-gluing route as the planned (not-yet-realized) target. See
1286	  % task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-ts216.md (must-fix).
1287	  The isomorphism is assembled by \emph{gluing canonical local isomorphisms}. The
1288	  construction rests on \emph{no} whiskering of the sheafification localizer,
1289	  \emph{no} instance \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and \emph{no} stalk
1290	  computation.
1291	
1292	  Fix a common open cover \(\{U\}\) of \(X\) (for instance an affine open cover).
1293	  On each member \(U\) form the canonical composite
1294	  \[
1295	    \bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\bigr)\big|_U
1296	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1297	    \bigl(M|_U \otimes_U N|_U\bigr) \otimes_U P|_U
1298	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1299	    M|_U \otimes_U \bigl(N|_U \otimes_U P|_U\bigr)
1300	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1301	    \bigl(M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\big|_U ,
1302	  \]
1303	  in which:
1304	  \begin{itemize}
1305	    \item the first arrow is two applications of the restriction-compatibility
1306	      isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} along the open immersion
1307	      \(U \hookrightarrow X\): first \((M \otimes_X N)|_U \cong M|_U \otimes_U
1308	      N|_U\) tensored with \(P|_U\), then \(\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X
1309	      P\bigr)|_U \cong (M \otimes_X N)|_U \otimes_U P|_U\);
1310	    \item the middle arrow is the \emph{canonical presheaf-level associator}
1311	      \(\alpha\) of the (symmetric) monoidal category
1312	      \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\)
1313	      (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\),
1314	      \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)), read on the restricted modules;
1315	    \item the third arrow is again two applications of
1316	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, in the opposite direction.
1317	  \end{itemize}
1318	  Each of the three arrows is a \emph{canonical} isomorphism: the
1319	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is induced
1320	  by the open immersion with no choices, hence natural in its module arguments, and
1321	  the presheaf associator \(\alpha\) is a fixed natural transformation. Therefore,
1322	  on a double overlap \(U \cap U'\), restricting the \(U\)-composite and the
1323	  \(U'\)-composite to \(U \cap U'\) yields the same morphism: each of the three
1324	  arrows commutes with the further restriction to \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1325	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the presheaf associator, so the two local
1326	  composites agree on the overlap.
1327	
1328	  Because the internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(-,-)\) of sheaves of
1329	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is itself a sheaf on \(X\), a family of morphisms defined
1330	  on the members of an open cover and agreeing on the overlaps glues to a single
1331	  global morphism. Applying this both to the local composites above and to their
1332	  local inverses produces a global morphism
1333	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \to M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\) whose restriction
1334	  to each \(U\) is the local composite, together with a two-sided inverse glued from
1335	  the local inverses; hence the glued morphism is a global isomorphism --- the
1336	  associator.
1337	
1338	  The single non-formal input is the restriction-compatibility comparison
1339	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the presheaf associator
1340	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)) and the sheaf-of-modules internal-hom gluing
1341	  are already available. Note that, unlike the pointwise-existence statement
1342	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} --- a \emph{proposition} proved
1343	  cover-pointwise with \emph{no} overlap obligation --- the associator is
1344	  \emph{global data}, a single isomorphism of sheaves on all of \(X\). Assembling it
1345	  from local pieces is therefore genuinely stronger than that pattern: the
1346	  overlap-naturality compatibility of the local composites must be discharged before
1347	  gluing, and is discharged above. The locally-trivial hypotheses of the statement
1348	  are not consumed.
1349	
1350	  \emph{The free-cover make-or-break.} The group law of
1351	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} consumes this associator only for
1352	  \emph{locally-trivial} modules (line bundles), so the cover \(\{U\}\) above may be
1353	  taken to \emph{trivialise} them: on each \(U\) the modules \(M, N, P\) restrict to
1354	  \emph{free} \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules. This is the whole point of building the
1355	  associator by gluing rather than by instantiating a coherent monoidal category. On a
1356	  free cover the two applications of the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
1357	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} that each local composite consumes are needed
1358	  \emph{only} in the cheap, structural case: where the modules are free,
1359	  sheaf-\(\otimes\) coincides on \(U\) with presheaf-\(\otimes\) (sheafification is the
1360	  identity on the already-sheafy free pieces), so the comparison is the elementary
1361	  base-change isomorphism of free modules along the open immersion, requiring no
1362	  general presheaf-pushforward adjunction. The PRIMARY proof obligation of this
1363	  construction is therefore the free-cover specialisation of
